quote: Great. This is Corey with Ion. I, gotten a request here you're looking at solar for your home. Is that right?
stewart_read: This is a Tesla-sourced lead — Greg's first substantive turn confirms it ('the ad I responded to said that Tesla was looking for people'). Corey uses the standard intro instead of the Tesla intro template. The Tesla intro ('we're the certified solar installer for Tesla in {state}') would have established the referral relationship immediately and pre-empted Greg's product expectation from forming unchecked. This is the upstream failure that makes the 00:21 and 03:11 moments structurally inevitable.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey may not have had lead-source metadata visible at call time, making the Tesla intro selection impossible rather than a choice. If lead-type metadata isn't surfaced in the dialer, this is a tooling gap, not a rep gap.
coaching_implication: Before coaching Corey on the 03:11 reframe, the manager should confirm whether Corey had visibility into the Tesla lead source at call time. If yes: drill the Tesla intro template and the trigger condition for using it. If no: escalate to ops — setters cannot select the right intro variant without lead-source data in the dialer.

quote: The ad I responded to said that Tesla was looking for people that, no. The ad kinda inferred that there was no cost involved.
stewart_read: Greg's self-correction mid-sentence ('Tesla was looking for people that, no') is the first signal of the Tesla roof-tile expectation. Corey hears only the 'no cost' surface concern and responds to that alone. The Tesla product signal is present and unaddressed. Strategic read: Corey may have judged the Tesla signal as too vague to address directly at this stage. Reactive read: Corey missed the signal entirely and defaulted to the cost clarification. The reactive read is more supported — there is no later move that picks up the Tesla thread before 03:11.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey deferred the Tesla reframe intentionally, planning to address it after verify when he had more context. Rejected — no evidence of a planned reframe; the 03:11 response confirms Corey had no reframe prepared.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to show Corey what the signal looks like in real time. The self-correction ('Tesla was looking for people that, no') is the tell. Drill the recognition pattern: when a customer references Tesla in the first 30 seconds, the Tesla relationship reframe is the immediate next move, not a deferral.

quote: I'll confirm because Thursday is Tuesday. Yes. There isn't any cost involved for, like, the installation. So there isn't any upfront cost, but that doesn't mean the equipment itself is free.
stewart_read: The first clause ('there isn't any cost involved for the installation') is schema-compliant legitimacy establishment — it addresses Greg's 'no cost' concern directly. The second clause ('but that doesn't mean the equipment itself is free') is the narrow coaching gap: it's an unsolicited disclosure that opens a cost-anxiety door Corey then has to manage. This is not scope creep by the schema definition (which covers closer-tier technical content), but it is minor over-disclosure — volunteering a potential objection before the customer raised it.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey may have judged that pre-empting the cost question was better than letting Greg discover it later and feel misled. That's a defensible instinct — but the execution ('that doesn't mean the equipment itself is free') frames it as a correction rather than a value bridge.
coaching_implication: The fix is not to remove the disclosure but to reframe it: instead of 'the equipment isn't free,' bridge immediately to the bill-swap value ('what you're replacing is your electricity bill, not adding a new expense'). The disclosure stays; the framing changes from correction to value.

quote: Well, you know, it varies greatly. It was almost, like, it was, like, $3.84 last month.
stewart_read: The transcript reads '$3.84 last month' — this is almost certainly a Deepgram transcription artifact for $384, but per the schema's numeric_discipline directive, this must be flagged explicitly: confirm against the actual bill or CRM record before using this number in any coaching language. Assuming the figure is $384: Corey's response ('Oh, jeez. Yeah. I can definitely see why you're looking into this') is an acknowledgment but not a flip. The bill is captured and dropped — the schema's 'ask + acknowledge + flip' pattern is truncated at acknowledge. No emotional alignment-against-the-utility is built.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey may have judged that the bill anchor was sufficient to carry momentum into the credit qualifier and bill collection. The call was moving smoothly at this point — a flip might have felt like over-engineering. Rejected: the flip is the mechanism that makes the bill pain sticky when the product expectation objection arrives at 03:11. Without it, Greg has no emotional reason to stay engaged when the Tesla news lands.
coaching_implication: Flag the transcription ambiguity first — confirm the actual bill amount. Then use this moment to drill the flip pattern: 'Oh, jeez' is the right instinct but the wrong landing. Suggested direction (not verbatim): acknowledge the pain, then redirect it toward the utility as the problem solar solves. The bill amount (once confirmed) is the anchor; the flip is what makes it stick.

quote: The ad showed that it was like Tesla, and these new panels look like shingles, actually. Or they weren't the ugly looking panels that sit on roofs. Are we still talking the same thing?
stewart_read: Greg is asking a product question, not expressing disinterest. He wants to know if what Ion offers matches what the ad showed. This is the moment the Tesla relationship reframe is required. The correct schema move: 'Tesla referred your inquiry to us — we're their installer partner in {state}. We install our own panels plus Tesla's Powerwall battery option. The roof-tile product isn't part of our catalog, but the specialist will walk you through what we do offer.' Corey instead confirms the mismatch and explains the back-order situation — accurate but framed as bad news with no alternative value offered.
coaching_implication: Drill the distinction between a product question and a withdrawal. At 03:11 Greg is still in the call — he's checking, not leaving. The reframe move here is the Tesla relationship clarification from the schema, not a rebuttal. The manager should role-play this exact moment: Greg asks 'are we still talking the same thing?' — what does Corey say? The answer should acknowledge the roof-tile gap, establish the referral relationship, and pivot to what Ion does offer. Remove the state reference from any coaching language unless the state is confirmed from the transcript or CRM — the transcript does not name a state.

quote: Unfortunately, not. So those have been back ordered for the better half of this decade. We're probably not gonna see them for a minute just because nobody has them right now with them being so far back ordered.
stewart_read: Corey's back-order explanation is factually plausible but strategically damaging. It frames the situation as 'the product you want doesn't exist anywhere' rather than 'Tesla referred you to us as their installer partner, and here's what we offer.' The schema is explicit: Ion sells its own panels plus the Tesla Powerwall battery — there is a real value proposition available. Corey either doesn't know the correct reframe or doesn't deploy it. The result is that Greg hears only what he can't have, not what he can.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey may have judged that confirming the back-order was the honest answer and that overselling Ion's offering would feel like a bait-and-switch. That instinct is correct in spirit but wrong in execution — the reframe isn't a bait-and-switch, it's an accurate description of the referral relationship.
coaching_implication: This is a knowledge gap, not a technique gap. Confirm whether Corey knows the Tesla referral relationship and Ion's actual product catalog (Ion panels + Tesla Powerwall). If not, that's a product-knowledge training item before any technique coaching. If yes, the coaching is on deployment: the reframe was available and unused.

quote: In that case, I'm probably not interested.
stewart_read: Greg's 'probably' is a soft withdrawal — the door is not fully closed. The schema's changed_mind rebuttal ('Just curious though — did you get a chance to look at the actual design with the numbers yet?') is the correct move here, combined with the objection_inversion pattern ('what you're not interested in is staying with what you have'). Corey exits immediately with no attempt. Strategic read: Corey judged the lead as unrecoverable given the product mismatch and chose not to waste time. Reactive read: Corey defaulted to the path of least resistance. The reactive read is more supported — 'probably' is explicit hedging, and the schema provides a direct tool for this exact moment.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey assessed that Greg's expectation was specifically for Tesla roof tiles and that no reframe would overcome a product-category mismatch. If Greg was only interested in the roof-tile product and nothing else, the call was genuinely unrecoverable. Rejected — Greg's 'probably' and the fact that he cooperated through the full verify sequence suggest he had broader interest that the product mismatch deflated, not a single-product-only requirement.
coaching_implication: Play the 03:40 moment in the 1-on-1 and ask Corey: what does 'probably' tell you? The answer should be 'the door is still open.' Then drill the changed_mind rebuttal and the objection_inversion pattern as the two tools available at this exact moment. The goal is not to guarantee a save — it's to ensure Corey never exits on a 'probably' without at least one curiosity-creation attempt.

quote: And is anybody else in the title of the home with you here, or is it just you?
stewart_read: Greg discloses at 01:57 that the title is held in a trust with their daughter's name on it. Corey's response is 'Okay. Great.' and moves immediately to the utility question. The schema's adult_child_decision_maker protocol is triggered: the daughter is on the title and is a co-decision-maker who must be included in the appointment. Corey captures the information and does not act on it. Since no appointment is ultimately booked, this doesn't create a structural vulnerability in this call — but it is a protocol miss that would have mattered if the call had reached button-up.
alternative_hypothesis: Corey may have judged that a trust arrangement with a daughter's name is a legal/estate structure rather than an active co-decision-maker situation, and that Greg is the functional decision-maker. That's a plausible read — but the schema's protocol is to confirm, not assume. The correct move is a brief clarifying question: 'Is your daughter involved in decisions about the home?'
coaching_implication: Use this as a protocol-recognition drill. The title-in-trust disclosure is a non-obvious trigger for the adult_child_decision_maker protocol. Corey needs to recognize that 'daughter's name on the title' = potential co-decision-maker = clarifying question required. This is lower priority than the Tesla reframe coaching but worth a brief mention in the 1-on-1.
